local_names.romanized_local: Ban Shu Dan (Chinese); Telur Separuh Masak (Malay)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for half-boiled egg; closest parent 'cooked egg food product' (FOODON_03301710) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Hen eggs, cooked) is the closest parent; no specific half-boiled egg code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 'Eggs (food)' is the closest available concept; no specific half-boiled egg concept exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of half-boiled eggs in Singapore is typically two medium hen eggs (approximately 100 g total), served in a small bowl with soy sauce and white pepper.

nutrient_source.title: Eggs, whole, cooked, hard-boiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01129); adapted for soft/half-boiled preparation
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for whole cooked hen egg used as primary reference. Half-boiled eggs have essentially identical macronutrient composition to hard-boiled eggs per 100 g edible portion; the only practical difference is water content distribution between white and yolk, which does not materially alter per-100 g macro values. Values cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN 2017 Table (Egg, hen, boiled) which reports similar values: energy 173 kcal, protein 13.3 g, fat 13.3 g, carbohydrate 0.0 g per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Eggs are a protein-fat food with negligible digestible carbohydrate (1.12 g per 100 g, of which the glycemic fraction is effectively zero). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature consistently classify eggs as having a GI of 0 (no measurable glycemic response). Per Veritas v2 scoring model requirements for foods with effectively zero GI, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and gi_category to 'low'. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (GI x carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (1 x 1.12) / 100 = 0.01.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (124 mg per 100 g); naturally low in added sodium when prepared plain
hypertension.guidance: Half-boiled eggs contain approximately 124 mg sodium per 100 g, which is moderate; individuals with hypertension should limit added soy sauce or salt at serving, as the traditional Singapore kopitiam condiment (dark soy sauce) can add 300–600 mg sodium per tablespoon.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible digestible carbohydrate (1.12 g per 100 g) with effectively zero glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Half-boiled eggs have a glycemic index of 1 (effectively zero) and contribute negligible carbohydrate load, making them an excellent protein-fat food choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; the high-quality protein (12.6 g per 100 g) supports satiety without raising postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (373 mg per 100 g) and moderate saturated fat (3.27 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Eggs are high in dietary cholesterol (373 mg per 100 g), and current evidence suggests moderate egg consumption (up to one egg per day) is acceptable for most individuals with cardiovascular disease, but those with familial hypercholesterolaemia or LDL-driven CVD risk should consult a clinician regarding frequency; the saturated fat content (3.27 g per 100 g) is moderate and should be considered within total daily saturated fat limits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Half-boiled egg (also called half-boil or soft-boiled egg in Singapore) is a classic preparation in which a whole hen egg is immersed in hot water for approximately three to four minutes, resulting in a fully set egg white and a warm, runny, custard-like yolk. In Singapore, this dish is a cornerstone of the kopitiam (traditional coffee shop) breakfast culture, typically served as a pair of eggs cracked into a small bowl, seasoned with a dash of dark soy sauce and white pepper, and eaten with kaya toast. The texture is silky and delicate, with the semi-liquid yolk providing a rich, creamy mouthfeel. Nutritionally, half-boiled eggs are an excellent source of high-quality complete protein (approximately 12.6 g per 100 g), healthy fats including monounsaturated fatty acids, and micronutrients such as vitamin B12, vitamin D, vitamin A, riboflavin, selenium, and choline. The carbohydrate content is negligible (approximately 1.12 g per 100 g), making this food suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic dietary patterns. The glycemic index is 1 (effectively zero). Cholesterol content is high (approximately 373 mg per 100 g), which is relevant for individuals with cardiovascular risk. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. In the Indian restaurant context (as served at Anjana Kitchen), half-boiled eggs may accompany curry or be served as a protein side dish alongside roti or rice.
common_user_queries: What is a half boil egg?
How many calories in a half boiled egg?
Is half boiled egg good for diabetes?
Can diabetics eat half boiled eggs?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked whole hen egg, NDB 01129) and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN 2017 Indian Food Composition Tables (Egg, hen, boiled). Both approved primary sources agree closely on core macros. Half-boiled egg is nutritionally equivalent to hard-boiled egg on a per-100 g basis. GI classification is well-established in peer-reviewed literature (eggs have negligible glycemic carbohydrate), with glycemic_index set to 1 per Veritas v2 scoring model requirements. Ontology codes are defensible closest-parent matches.
notes: 1. The food_name has been expanded from 'Half Boil' to 'Half Boil Egg' for canonical clarity; 'Half Boil' is retained as the primary English alias. 2. Nutrient values are from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked whole egg) adapted for half-boiled preparation; per-100 g macros are materially identical between hard-boiled and half-boiled eggs. ICMR-NIN 2017 cross-reference confirms values within acceptable range. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: eggs contain no dietary fibre by nature (naturally zero). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: eggs contain negligible vitamin C (naturally zero per USDA FoodData Central). 4. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) per Veritas v2 scoring model requirements for foods with negligible glycemic carbohydrate, even though scientific literature reports GI=0 for eggs. gi_category is 'low' (consistent with GI <= 55). glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.01, calculated as (GI x carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (1 x 1.12) / 100. This aligns with the critical rule that glycemic_index must be greater than zero. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are all reported from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and are not -1. 6. The input listed cuisine as 'Indian' and the serving restaurant as Anjana Kitchen (Indian cuisine); this is preserved. Half-boiled eggs are also a quintessential Singaporean kopitiam dish, so multilingual coverage follows Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). 7. Price data from field survey (SGD 9.75) is noted from input but not included in the RESULT template as it is not a template field. 8. Aliases consistency verified (iteration 3 fix): all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases list. The previous iteration contained 'ஆधा उबला अंडा' (a mixed-script corruption) in the flat aliases list instead of the correct Hindi string 'आधा उबला अंडा'. This has been corrected so that the flat aliases list contains the exact string 'आधा उबला अंडा' matching the hindi entry in aliases_by_language.

improvement_actions_taken: Expanded food_name from 'Half Boil' to 'Half Boil Egg' for canonical precision; retained 'Half Boil' as primary English alias.
Added full multilingual local_names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish rules.
Sourced nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01129) and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN 2017.
Set GI = 1 with gi_category = 'low' per Veritas v2 scoring model requirements for effectively zero-GI foods; documented in notes and gi_source.
Added defensible ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2, SNOMED CT) as closest_parent matches with gap notes.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Wrote natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters with full cultural and clinical context.
Documented all zero values (dietary_fibre_g, vitamin_c_mg) with 'naturally zero' / 'confirmed zero' justification in notes.
Confirmed no -1 sentinel values remain undocumented.
ITERATION 2 ACTION: Corrected glycemic_index from 0 to 1 and glycemic_load_per_serving from 0.0 to 0.01 to comply with the critical rule that glycemic_index must be greater than zero.

ITERATION 3 ACTION: Fixed q8 failure — the previous flat aliases list contained 'ஆधा उबला अंडा' (a mixed-script corruption of the Hindi string) instead of the correct pure Devanagari string 'आधा उबला अंडा'. Replaced the corrupted entry with the exact string 'आधा उबला अंडा' to match the aliases_by_language hindi entry verbatim. All aliases_by_language entries now verified to appear character-for-character in the flat aliases array.
